evals.prompt: I'm building a tool called Pulsewatch. It's a server-side observability product for small dev teams (3–15 engineers) — you drop one agent on your VPS and you immediately see logs, metrics, traces, and alerts unified into a single timeline. The pitch is 'Datadog without the bill, set up in 90 seconds'. Audience is bootstrapped startup CTOs and indie devs. I want to see a few visual directions before I commit. Output to ./pulsewatch-styles/ and tell me how to view them.
evals.expected_output: 3-5 self-contained HTML variants in ./pulsewatch-styles/<NN-style-slug>/index.html, plus a comparison index.html generated by the bundled script. Variants should be visually distinct (not three flavors of one style), use the actual product copy (Pulsewatch, observability messaging) not lorem ipsum, and lean toward dev/SaaS-appropriate styles.

evals.prompt: Help me explore design directions for Mira — a luxury Italian leather goods e-commerce brand launching in late 2026. Hero collection is hand-stitched bags priced $800-$3500. Audience is women 30-55 who shop at Loro Piana and The Row. The site needs to feel quiet, expensive, and deeply considered. Generate a few options into ~/desktop/mira-design and let me know what's there.
evals.expected_output: 3-5 self-contained HTML variants in ~/desktop/mira-design/<NN-style-slug>/index.html. Variants should be appropriate for luxury e-commerce — editorial / minimal / dark — and explicitly NOT include loud styles (Brutalism, Cyberpunk, Y2K) which would erode the brand. Real product copy, comparison index generated.

evals.prompt: I have a one-pager for an AI app I'm prototyping called 'Loom'. Loom takes a recording of any meeting and generates a structured doc with decisions, action items, and a chronological narrative. For PMs and consultants. I genuinely don't know what it should look like — I want to see a wide range of directions including some I might reject, so I can figure out my taste. Put files in ./loom-prototypes.
evals.expected_output: 3-5 self-contained HTML variants in ./loom-prototypes/<NN-style-slug>/index.html. Should span the full design space including at least one safe option (Minimalism/Modern Dark) AND at least one bold/wildcard (Brutalism, Cyberpunk, or similar). Comparison index generated. The variants should be intentionally diverse to help the user discover their own taste.

evals.prompt: Modern Dark 那个最对味 — go deeper on #2 and give me 4 derivatives in that direction. Output dir is the same one as last time: ./pulsewatch-styles/. I want to feel which sub-direction inside Modern Dark works best.
evals.expected_output: Mode B refinement run. The skill detects state.json, identifies Modern Dark as the picked variant from batch-1, and generates 4 new variants under ./pulsewatch-styles/batch-2/ that vary along Modern Dark's axes (accent color / type system / density / hero pattern / surface depth) while keeping the locked shared_copy verbatim. State.json is updated with a new batch entry recording the refinement lineage. Subagent prompts must read the reference variant before writing.
evals.setup: Pre-populate ./pulsewatch-styles/ with: (a) state.json containing product.name='Pulsewatch', a populated shared_copy block (headline, subhead, 3 features, cta_primary, cta_secondary, footer), and batches[0] with kind='fresh', dir='batch-1', styles=['Minimalism','Modern Dark','Bento Grid','Neubrutalism']; (b) batch-1/02-modern-dark/index.html as a minimal but valid Modern Dark placeholder using that shared_copy verbatim; (c) batch-1/index.html comparison page. No batch-2/ yet.
expectations: Skill detects existing state.json and follows Mode B (does NOT overwrite batch-1/ or generate fresh-different styles).
All new variants are written under ./pulsewatch-styles/batch-2/, not batch-1/.
At least 4 variant directories exist under batch-2/ (e.g. batch-2/01-*, 02-*, 03-*, 04-*), each with index.html.
state.json is updated so batches[1].kind equals 'refinement' (the canonical Mode B value per the kind-enum table in references/iteration-modes.md).
state.json batches[1].based_on is a list containing 'Modern Dark'.
state.json batches[1].dir equals 'batch-2'.
Subagent prompts dispatched for each refinement variant include the literal substring 'Reference variant (read first):' followed by the path to batch-1/02-modern-dark/index.html.
The locked shared_copy (headline, subhead, all 3 features, cta_primary) appears verbatim in every one of the 4 new variants — no paraphrasing across variants.
Variant slug names encode the axis combination (e.g. 01-modern-dark-blue-spacious, 02-modern-dark-violet-dense) so the user can skim them.
serve_preview.py is invoked on the OUTPUT ROOT (./pulsewatch-styles/) and not on batch-2/ directly, so the top-level tabbed index is regenerated.

evals.prompt: I'm building Ledgerline — a payments reconciliation dashboard for finance ops at Series B-D startups. I want it to feel like https://stripe.com/ and https://linear.app/ — clean, confident, a little bit of subtle gradient identity. Give me 5 variants in that visual family. Output to ./ledgerline-design/.
evals.expected_output: Mode C reference-driven run. The skill runs extract_brand_dna.py on the two URLs first, persists the resulting DNA, then generates 5 variants that all live inside that DNA but vary along sub-axes (tonal range / hero device / density / decorative weight / trust positioning). All 5 share the same primary accent and typeface family — the differences should feel like '5 designers in the same studio'. state.json records the reference lineage and the DNA summary.
expectations: extract_brand_dna.py is invoked with both https://stripe.com/ and https://linear.app/ as positional arguments and writes a dna.json (or equivalent) under ./ledgerline-design/.
The skill does NOT use WebFetch to prose-summarize the reference pages, and does NOT manually grep the HTML for hex colors instead of running the script.

The generated dna.json contains a non-empty 'solids' list with at least one #RRGGBB value, a non-empty 'gradients' list with at least one gradient entry that has 'stops' populated, and a non-empty 'fonts' (or 'typography') field.
At least 5 variant directories exist under ./ledgerline-design/batch-1/ (e.g. batch-1/01-*, 02-*, 03-*, 04-*, 05-*), each with index.html.
Every one of the 5 variant index.html files contains at least one of the brand hex colors extracted into dna.json, used inside an actual CSS rule (not just in a code-block snippet).
state.json batches[0].kind equals 'reference-driven'.
state.json batches[0].based_on is a list containing both URLs ('https://stripe.com/' and 'https://linear.app/').
state.json batches[0].reference_summary is a non-empty string that mentions either Stripe or Linear by name and references colors or typography.
Variant slugs are sibling-named (e.g. 01-stripe-clean, 02-linear-spare) rather than abstract style-catalog names.
Locked shared_copy (Ledgerline product name, headline, subhead, features, CTA) appears verbatim in every variant.

evals.prompt: 锁定 #2 — go with the second one (the one with the linear-gradient hero). Emit DESIGN.md for it. Product name is Ledgerline, one-line pitch: 'Payments reconciliation for finance ops at Series B-D startups.'
evals.expected_output: extract_design_md.py runs against the chosen variant. The resulting DESIGN.md has YAML front-matter that includes both a colors: block AND a gradients: block (because the variant has a linear-gradient declaration). Solid hex values that are also gradient stops are renamed with -start / -end / -mid suffixes (not 'accent' / 'highlight'). The front-matter is in the getdesign.md / Stitch extended canonical shape (full typography hierarchy with lineHeight/letterSpacing, named spacing scale, rounded incl pill/full, a components: map with {brace} refs). The 11-section markdown body has all <!-- LLM-FILL: --> placeholders replaced by real, variant-specific prose grounded in what is actually on the page.
evals.setup: Pre-populate ./ledgerline-design/batch-1/02-linear-gradient-hero/index.html with a self-contained variant that uses Ledgerline's locked copy and contains at minimum: (a) a hero section with `background: linear-gradient(135deg, #5B7FFF 0%, #A78BFA 100%)` (or equivalent inline-style), (b) at least 2 Google Fonts referenced via fonts.googleapis.com, (c) a primary CTA button with its own solid color, (d) a footer with one more solid color. Also pre-populate ./ledgerline-design/state.json with product, shared_copy, and batches[0] referencing this variant.
expectations: extract_design_md.py is invoked with the variant directory path and --name 'Ledgerline'.

DESIGN.md is written to extract_design_md.py's default location: the git repo root (git rev-parse --show-toplevel on the variant dir) when the variant is inside a git repo, otherwise the variant's parent dir (<output>/batch-1/DESIGN.md). The harness must accept the repo-root location — SKILL.md step 7 deliberately passes no --output, and repo root is the intended default (DESIGN.md is the only committed artifact). Do NOT require ./ledgerline-design/batch-1/DESIGN.md specifically.
The YAML front-matter contains a top-level 'gradients:' key (not just 'colors:').
At least one gradient entry in the YAML has direction '135deg' and contains both stop colors (case-insensitive match for #5B7FFF and #A78BFA).

The 'colors:' block does NOT contain a token literally named 'accent' or 'highlight' for #5B7FFF or #A78BFA — those hexes are renamed with a -start / -end / -mid suffix tying them to their gradient (e.g. brand-start, brand-end).
Each renamed gradient-stop solid carries a trailing comment of the form '# gradients.<name> stop <i>'.

All 11 canonical sections are present in the markdown body as plain '## Title' headings (no numeric prefix), in order: Overview, Colors, Typography, Layout, Elevation & Depth, Shapes, Components, Do's and Don'ts, Responsive Behavior, Iteration Guide, Known Gaps — matching references/design-md-spec.md.

The YAML front-matter contains a 'typography:' block with the full named hierarchy (display-xl … nav-link) where each entry has fontFamily/fontSize/fontWeight/lineHeight/letterSpacing, a 'spacing:' block keyed by the named scale (xxs … section), a 'rounded:' block that includes pill and full, and a 'components:' block whose values reference tokens via {brace} syntax (e.g. "{colors.accent}", "{typography.button}").
After the calling agent's prose-fill pass, the file contains zero remaining `<!-- LLM-FILL:` substrings.
(Optional / human review) The prose in each section is grounded in the actual variant — references the gradient, the chosen typefaces, the CTA color etc. rather than generic design-system filler.
(Optional) `npx @google/design.md lint <path>/DESIGN.md` exits 0.

evals.prompt: I have a variant at ./broken-output/batch-1/03-suspicious/index.html that something feels off about. Run validate_variant.py on it and tell me what's wrong.
evals.expected_output: Defensive validation eval. validate_variant.py runs against the prepared bad variant, detects the deliberately-seeded defects (lorem ipsum present, brand name missing), writes a validation.json with per-check evidence, and exits non-zero. The skill surfaces the failures to the user with the specific evidence strings.
evals.setup: Pre-populate ./broken-output/ with: (a) state.json where product.name='Halcyon' and shared_copy.headline / subhead / cta_primary are all populated with real strings; (b) batch-1/03-suspicious/index.html that is a syntactically valid self-contained HTML page over 5KB with a <style> block, BUT (i) contains the substring 'lorem ipsum' twice in body copy, (ii) does NOT contain the brand name 'Halcyon' anywhere in rendered text, (iii) otherwise uses the locked headline/subhead/CTA verbatim so other checks pass.
expectations: validate_variant.py is invoked with ./broken-output/batch-1/03-suspicious/ as the variant_dir argument.
The script discovers ./broken-output/state.json automatically (or the user passes --state) — it does not skip the state-dependent checks.
The 'no-lorem-ipsum' check fails with evidence string '2 occurrences'.
The 'brand-name-present' check fails with evidence indicating 0 occurrences of 'Halcyon'.
The 'has-style-block', 'min-size', 'html-parses', 'has-cta-text', 'headline-verbatim', and 'subhead-verbatim' checks all pass (so the failures are isolated to the seeded defects).
Overall summary is FAIL: summary.overall_pass is false and summary.errors_failed is at least 2.
validate_variant.py exits with a non-zero exit code.
./broken-output/batch-1/03-suspicious/validation.json is written and is valid JSON with the per-check records and the summary block.

evals.prompt: Give me 4 more variants in different directions for this product — output is in ./old-output/. Last time was a while ago.
evals.expected_output: Migration + Mode A run. The skill detects that ./old-output/ uses the legacy flat layout (no state.json, no batch-1/ wrapper), runs init_iteration.py to migrate it to batched layout (preferably running --commit only after a dry-run / user-acknowledged plan, per init_iteration's design), then generates batch-2 with 4 fresh-different variants on top of the migrated structure. State.json is created during migration and extended after batch-2 generation.
evals.setup: Pre-populate ./old-output/ in the FLAT layout: (a) ./old-output/01-minimalism/index.html, (b) ./old-output/02-bento-grid/index.html, (c) ./old-output/03-modern-dark/index.html — each a self-contained HTML page with shared product copy for product 'Northwind' (a hypothetical shipping-API product). Also include ./old-output/index.html (old comparison page) and ./old-output/SUMMARY.txt. NO state.json, NO batch-1/ wrapper, NO batch-N/ subdirs.
expectations: Skill detects the flat layout BEFORE attempting to write batch-2 (does not create batch-2/ next to flat 01-*/02-*/03-* directories).
init_iteration.py is invoked with ./old-output/ and --name 'Northwind'; --commit is passed (after a dry-run plan was reviewed, or with explicit user confirmation in-prompt).
After migration, ./old-output/01-minimalism/, /02-bento-grid/, and /03-modern-dark/ no longer exist at the root — they are now under ./old-output/batch-1/.
After migration, the original ./old-output/index.html is now at ./old-output/batch-1/index.html (and ./old-output/SUMMARY.txt is at ./old-output/batch-1/SUMMARY.txt).

./old-output/state.json exists after migration and parses as JSON with batches[0].dir == 'batch-1', batches[0].kind == 'fresh', and batches[0].styles containing all three migrated style display names ('Minimalism', 'Bento Grid', 'Modern Dark').
After migration, batch-2 is generated at ./old-output/batch-2/ with 4 new variant subdirectories, each with index.html.
batch-2 styles are NOT a repeat of any style from batch-1 (exclusion list honored — no second Minimalism / Bento Grid / Modern Dark).
state.json is updated post-batch-2 to include batches[1] with dir=='batch-2' and kind=='fresh-different' (the canonical Mode A value per the kind-enum table in references/iteration-modes.md).
serve_preview.py is run on the OUTPUT ROOT (./old-output/) so the new top-level tabbed index covers both batch-1 and batch-2.

evals.prompt: Bento 那版就定了 — now I want to feel different page layouts under it. 再换几种排版:single-column long scroll、bento 9 格、sidebar workspace、还有一个 pricing 对比页。Output dir 还是 ./atlas-design/.
evals.expected_output: Mode D layout-exploration run. The skill detects state.json, confirms a style winner was already picked (Bento Grid), locks it, and generates 4–6 variants under ./atlas-design/batch-2/ that vary ONLY the page layout while holding the locked Bento style constant (same fonts, accent, button/card identity from DESIGN.md). It computes the content gap for the chosen layouts, expands shared_copy ONCE (adding pricing_tiers / features_extended without rewriting the locked original 4 fields), confirms the expansion with a single yes/no message, and records the lineage in state.json. The validator runs in layout mode.
evals.setup: Pre-populate ./atlas-design/ with: (a) state.json where product.name='Atlas', shared_copy has the locked original four fields (headline, subhead, features[3], cta_primary) populated, batches[0] kind='fresh' dir='batch-1' styles=['Minimalism','Bento Grid','Modern Dark','Brutalism'] user_picks=['Bento Grid']; (b) batch-1/02-bento-grid/index.html as a valid Bento variant using that shared_copy verbatim; (c) batch-1/index.html; (d) a DESIGN.md at the repo root extracted from the Bento variant (colors/typography/spacing tokens). No batch-2/ yet.
expectations: Skill detects state.json, identifies Bento Grid as the already-picked style winner from batch-1, and does NOT run an interview or re-pick fresh styles.
All new variants are written under ./atlas-design/batch-2/, not batch-1/.

At least 4 variant directories exist under batch-2/ with slugs matching layout-catalog names (e.g. 01-single-column-long-scroll, 02-bento-9-tile, 03-sidebar-workspace, 04-hero-pricing-comparison), each with index.html.
state.json batches[1].kind equals 'layout' (the canonical Mode D value per the kind-enum table in references/iteration-modes.md).

state.json batches[1].locked_style.source_variant points at batch-1/02-bento-grid (or equivalent picked-variant path) and locked_style.style_name names Bento Grid; based_on is a list containing the Bento style name.
state.json batches[1].layout_axes is a list of the layout slugs, and batches[1].styles is the human-readable layout names.

shared_copy is expanded append-only: the original headline / subhead / features[0..2] / cta_primary are byte-identical to batch-1's, and any new fields (pricing_tiers, features_extended) are ADDED with a shared_copy.expansions audit entry recording added_in_batch and source — the original fields are NOT rewritten.

Every Mode D subagent prompt instructs the agent to read state.json shared_copy verbatim, read the repo-root DESIGN.md tokens verbatim, AND read the reference variant batch-1/02-bento-grid/index.html for style fidelity, varying ONLY the layout.

validate_variant.py is run on each batch-2 variant and auto-detects layout mode (batches[1].kind=='layout'), enabling the extended pricing/features-extended verbatim checks; the locked headline/subhead/cta_primary still appear verbatim in every variant.
serve_preview.py is invoked on the OUTPUT ROOT (./atlas-design/) so the tabbed root index gains a LAYOUT tab alongside the batch-1 style tab.
